headline: BaseballPro77 Shatters the Home Run Record and Announces Itself as a Legitimate Title Contender
subheadline: A historic 19-home run week, a league-record-tying 39 RBI, and a 7-2 demolition of the Wisconsin Rapids Reapers signal that the balance of power in MillerLite® BeerLeagueBaseball may be shifting.
body:
## Thriller of the Week

### Canberra Wombats vs. Riverside Roughnecks — 4-4

There are ties in fantasy baseball that feel inevitable, and then there are ties that sting — the kind where both managers spend Sunday night convinced they deserved more. The **Canberra Wombats** and **Riverside Roughnecks** produced exactly that variety of stalemate in Week Four, splitting eight categories in a matchup that was closer than a box score can adequately convey. The **Roughnecks** brought a league-leading .368 OBP and a stout bullpen headlined by Misiorowski's 14 strikeouts, while the **Wombats** countered with Jo Adell's power production and Pete Crow-Armstrong providing life at the top of the lineup. Neither team was able to establish separation in the categories that matter most, and the final 4-4 ledger left both rosters in the same standing they occupied entering the week. The frustration is compounded by context: both clubs had reasonable paths to a full win this week, and both will now enter Week Five needing a genuine result. The **Roughnecks** face the **Athletic Park Dragons** in what projects as one of the more competitive matchups on the slate. The **Wombats**, meanwhile, must live with the consequences of a tie that does nothing to distance them from the lower half of the standings.

---

## The Week's Defining Moment

It has become something of a ritual in competitive fantasy leagues — a single week where one roster assembles a performance so complete, so overwhelming in its totality, that it reframes every conversation about the season. Week Four of MillerLite® BeerLeagueBaseball belonged to **BaseballPro77**, and it was not particularly close.

The final margin was 7-2 over the **Wisconsin Rapids Reapers**, but the numbers beneath that score are what demand attention. Nineteen home runs. Thirty-nine runs batted in. A staff ERA of 2.33. This was not a team catching a hot week — this was a roster operating at a level that the league had not previously witnessed. Mike Trout contributed five home runs and nine RBI, the kind of individual output that would anchor most winning weeks by itself. Munetaka Murakami added four home runs and nine RBI of his own, and Austin Riley supplied three home runs and nine RBI. All three of those performances, in another week, in another matchup, would be the dominant storyline. Here, they were simply components of something larger.

The pitching staff was every bit the equal of the offense. Gavin Williams and George Kirby anchored a rotation that held the **Reapers** to 33 hits, a .257 OBP, and 27 runs — respectable totals in isolation, but wholly overshadowed by what the **BaseballPro77** lineup was doing on the other side of the ledger. The **Reapers** were not disgraced; Bryan Woo contributed two quality starts and Mason Miller delivered four saves/holds. They simply had the misfortune of drawing the hottest team in the league during its finest week.

What makes this performance consequential beyond the record books is what it suggests about **BaseballPro77**'s ceiling. The **Sugar Land Skeeters** have been the league's measuring stick through four weeks — consistent, disciplined, difficult to beat in any single category. But this week, **BaseballPro77** served notice that a challenger has arrived. The two clubs are now, by any reasonable measure, the teams to beat as the season approaches its midpoint.

---

## Matchup Deep-Dives

### BaseballPro77 def. Wisconsin Rapids Reapers, 7-2

The dominant story of Week Four requires no embellishment. **BaseballPro77** won seven of nine categories available, surrendering only stolen bases and NSVH to a **Reapers** club that, in fairness, managed to salvage dignity where it could. The offensive production was historic — more on that in a dedicated section below — and the pitching complemented it with a 2.33 ERA that led the entire league this week. The **Reapers** are not a lost cause; Mason Miller's nine NSVH led the league, and Bryan Woo's dual quality starts represent genuine rotation value. However, the decision to drop McGreevy without a viable replacement leaves the staff dangerously thin at a moment when the schedule does not invite experimentation. For **BaseballPro77**, the challenge now shifts from proving it can dominate to proving it can sustain. A Week Five matchup against the **Abbotsford-Colby Twins** will provide immediate clarity.

### General Hospital def. Benchwarmers, 8-2

There are wins that flatter, and then there are wins that confirm. **General Hospital**'s 8-2 dismantling of the **Benchwarmers** falls into the latter category. The rotation posted a 2.68 ERA, a league-leading 0.96 WHIP, and six quality starts — the kind of pitching line that wins fantasy matchups regardless of what the offense produces. The offense produced anyway, with 36 RBI ranking second in the league on the week. The **Benchwarmers** were not without their bright spots — Michael King's ERA was outstanding, and Cody Bellinger showed signs of life — but four home runs, 45 strikeouts, and a lineup that lacks consistent power are structural problems that a single good week from King cannot solve. The addition of Kumar Rocker to an already formidable **General Hospital** rotation is the kind of move that shifts a team from contender to genuine threat. They were already scary. Now they are something more concerning than that.

### Albuquerque Bombers def. Sugar Land Skeeters, 5-4

The most surprising result of the week may well be this one. The **Sugar Land Skeeters** entered as the most consistently excellent team in the league, and they remain that — but the **Albuquerque Bombers** were better in Week Four. Byron Buxton and DJ Stewart each contributed three home runs, and Nick Turang's .429 OBP provided the kind of table-setting that turns runs into crooked numbers. The **Bombers** scored 40 runs — the most of any team in the league this week — and the final margin of 5-4 does not fully capture how thoroughly they outperformed the opposition in the counting categories. The **Skeeters** were not without merit: their 72 strikeouts led the entire league, and a 4 QS performance suggests the pitching remains sound. The ERA of 6.05, however, is an anomaly that this staff is too talented to repeat. The **Bombers**' four waiver additions this week signal a manager who views this as a window, not a fluke. The **Skeeters**, meanwhile, face North Correa in what should be a clarifying matchup in Week Five.

### Abbotsford-Colby Twins def. Hiroshima Radiation, 6-4

This matchup carried genuine intrigue throughout the week, and the final score understates how close it remained. The **Twins** won six categories on the strength of what may have been the finest individual performance of the season — José Ramírez put together a week that included four home runs, five stolen bases, and a .533 OBP, a combination that is nearly impossible to assemble. The **Twins** also led the league in stolen bases and finished second in home runs. And yet the margin was only 6-4, which speaks to how well the **Hiroshima Radiation** pitched. Tarik Skubal and Nathan Eovaldi each posted ERA figures below 1.50, anchoring a staff ERA of 2.81 with five quality starts. The **Radiation** lost this matchup because of a negative NSVH — a structural problem in the saves/holds department that is not easily corrected mid-season. That weakness becomes more acute in Week Five against **General Hospital**, which is pitching at an elite level right now.

### Athletic Park Dragons def. North Correa, 5-2

**Athletic Park** looked every bit the top-five team the power rankings suggest it is. Aaron Judge's five home runs were the centerpiece of a well-rounded offensive effort, and the staff ERA of 3.01 was efficient enough to protect a lead across most categories. The **Dragons** tied for the league lead in stolen bases with eight, adding a dimension to their game that their power numbers alone do not reveal. **North Correa** had its moments — Nico Hoerner delivered 11 RBI in one of the strongest individual weeks at the position this season, and the catcher upgrade from J.T. Realmuto to Austin Wells is a smart, forward-looking decision. The **Correa** pitching staff, however, surrendered a 5.56 ERA, which is unsustainable against quality competition. The measuring-stick moment arrives in Week Five when **North Correa** faces **Sugar Land** — a game that could define the contender vs. pretender conversation for both clubs.

### Wonder Wharf Wonderdogs def. Buttville Skidmarks, 6-3

The **Wonder Wharf Wonderdogs** continue to be one of the most quietly impressive teams in this league. Eight quality starts — the most in the league this week by a significant margin — anchored a 2.49 ERA and a 0.98 WHIP, and Paul Skenes' 0.90 ERA was the kind of individual outing that wins a category by itself. The offense was functional rather than explosive, but functional was sufficient against a **Buttville Skidmarks** team dealing with significant pitching liabilities. MacKenzie Gore's 6.52 ERA in the rotation is a problem that will not age well, and a 4.87 team ERA in an era when pitching depth separates contenders from aspirants is a genuine ceiling. Junior Caminero's three home runs and Royce Langford's .450 OBP show that the **Skidmarks** offense has moments of real quality. The rotation needs to find stability before those moments can translate into wins against better competition.

---

## Lucky and Unlucky Team of the Week

**Lucky Team: Benchwarmers**

The **Benchwarmers** won two more categories this week than their underlying production profile suggested they should. Four home runs, a 4.40 ERA, and just 45 strikeouts represent one of the weakest composite performances of any team in Week Four, yet the final ledger looked more respectable than those figures deserve. Michael King was genuinely excellent, and that excellence carried more weight than it should have in a week when the rest of the roster offered little. The **Benchwarmers** face the **Buttville Skidmarks** in Week Five — a matchup that is legitimately winnable — but the manager should understand that this team's recent results have overstated its true quality. The power profile, in particular, cannot continue to underperform without consequences.

**Unlucky Team: Abbotsford-Colby Twins**

The **Twins** won their matchup 6-4 and received an extraordinary performance from their best player. Yet their luck delta of -0.61 is the worst in the league, meaning that across the full season, their category production has repeatedly exceeded what their actual win total reflects. This week's output — 14 home runs (second in the league), eight stolen bases (tied for the league lead), and the Ramírez masterpiece — deserved a wider margin than it produced. The **Twins** are a better team than their record communicates, and that distinction will matter when the schedule grows more difficult. Week Five against **BaseballPro77** is as difficult as it gets.

---

## League Records

The record books required updating on multiple fronts this week. **BaseballPro77** set a new single-week team home run record with 19 home runs, establishing a new standard for what this league's most dangerous offensive roster can produce in a seven-day span. The previous mark was 19.0 — this performance now stands alone. Simultaneously, **BaseballPro77** tied the all-time single-week RBI record of 39, matching their own mark set earlier this season. That they hold both ends of the record is a statement about the sustained excellence of this lineup. These are not aberrations. They are patterns.

---

## Manager Spotlight: North Correa

**North Correa** entered Week Four ranked eighth in the power rankings, and a 5-2 loss to the **Athletic Park Dragons** did nothing to alter that assessment — though not because the performance was poor. This is a team that continues to make sound, forward-looking decisions. The replacement of J.T. Realmuto with Austin Wells at catcher is precisely the kind of positional upgrade that separates managers who react from managers who anticipate. Realmuto has been a reliable fantasy name for years; Wells is the name that matters now, and **North Correa** identified that reality and acted on it.

Nico Hoerner's 11 RBI was among the finest individual week-four performances in the league, and the pitching staff demonstrated the capability of a quality-start rotation even in a losing effort. The ERA of 5.56 is a legitimate concern, but the underlying structure — five quality starts, competitive strikeout numbers — suggests a staff that had a difficult week rather than a fundamentally flawed one.

The question of how far **North Correa** can go this season will begin to find its answer in Week Five. A matchup against the **Sugar Land Skeeters** is a true measuring-stick game — the kind that tells a manager whether his roster belongs in a conversation about winning championships or needs additional construction before that conversation is justified. **North Correa** has the pieces. Week Five will reveal whether the sum of those pieces is greater than the parts.

---

## Trade Value Watch

**José Ramírez (Abbotsford-Colby Twins):** Ramírez delivered four home runs, five stolen bases, and a .533 OBP in one of the most complete individual performances of the season — a reminder that when he is locked in, he is the most valuable multi-category asset in this league. **Verdict: Hold.** There is no logical reason to sell the most complete third baseman in fantasy baseball following one of his best weeks of the season, and his combination of power and speed makes him irreplaceable in a category-scoring format. Any manager considering an offer should expect to significantly overpay.

**Paul Skenes (Wonder Wharf Wonderdogs):** Skenes posted a 0.90 ERA as part of a **Wonderdogs** rotation that collectively produced eight quality starts and a 2.49 ERA — the second-best team ERA in the league this week. He is pitching like an ace, not a prospect. **Verdict: Buy, if available.** Managers who do not own Skenes should be making aggressive offers. His combination of strikeout upside and rate-stat dominance makes him one of the most valuable pitching assets in the league at his current level of performance.

**Aaron Judge (Athletic Park Dragons):** Judge's five home runs anchored a clean **Athletic Park** win, continuing a pattern of power production that has defined this team's offensive identity. **Verdict: Hold.** Judge remains among the most feared hitters in the league on a weekly basis. His value is not in question — the only question is whether **Athletic Park** has sufficient complementary pieces to build a championship around him. Through four weeks, the answer appears to be yes.

---

## Power Rankings

1. **BaseballPro77** | Record-setting 19 HR and 39 RBI with a 2.33 ERA — this team just issued the loudest statement of the season and now faces Abbotsford-Colby Twins in a massive week-five test [UP]
2. **Sugar Land Skeeters** | Still the most consistent team in the league across five weeks and a top-four category-wins finisher even in a loss; North Correa awaits in what could be the marquee matchup of week five [SAME]
3. **General Hospital** | An 8-2 demolition backed by a 2.68 ERA, 6 QS, and a 0.96 WHIP confirms this rotation is elite; Kumar Rocker's addition makes an already-scary staff downright unfair [UP]
4. **Wonder Wharf Wonderdogs** | Six quality starts, a 2.49 ERA, and Paul Skenes posting a 0.90 ERA — the pitching is legitimately elite and the offense showed enough life to earn a 6-3 win; the -0.41 luck delta remains a nagging concern [UP]
5. **Athletic Park Dragons** | Aaron Judge's 5 HR and a 3.01 ERA anchored a clean 5-2 win; this team is rounding into form and faces Riverside Roughnecks in a pivotal week-five clash [SAME]
6. **Albuquerque Bombers** | A 5-4 win fueled by Buxton (3 HR), Stewart (3 HR), and Turang's .429 OBP shows a roster firing on multiple cylinders; four waiver adds this week signal an aggressive manager not satisfied with good enough [SAME]
7. **Hiroshima Radiation** | Skubal and Eovaldi each posted sub-1.50 ERAs and the team ERA of 2.81 with 5 QS is elite, but a negative NSVH dragged them to a 6-4 loss — the saves situation is a genuine structural weakness heading into General Hospital [DOWN]
8. **North Correa** | Nico Hoerner's 11 RBI was one of the best individual performances of the week and Austin Wells replaces Realmuto at catcher in a smart positional upgrade; a date with Sugar Land looms as a true measuring-stick game [SAME]
9. **Abbotsford-Colby Twins** | José Ramírez went 4 HR, 5 SB, and a .533 OBP in one of the best individual weeks of the season and the Twins still nearly let Hiroshima back in — a murderous week-five matchup against BaseballPro77 will reveal a lot [DOWN]
10. **Riverside Roughnecks** | A 4-4 tie with a weaker Canberra team is a missed opportunity; Misiorowski's 14 K and a loaded bullpen are real assets but the offense needs Kurtz and Story to deliver consistently heading into an Athletic Park showdown [SAME]
11. **Canberra Wombats** | Jo Adell (2 HR, 8 RBI) and Pete Crow-Armstrong provided offense but a 4.35 ERA and just 52 K in under 50 innings is below average; dropping Roki Sasaki for JR Ritchie is a significant gamble that could define their season [UP]
12. **Buttville Skidmarks** | Junior Caminero delivered 3 HR and Langford hit .450 OBP but a 4.87 ERA and Gore's 6.52 ERA in the rotation are crippling; the offense has moments but too many pitching liabilities to move up the board [SAME]
13. **Wisconsin Rapids Reapers** | Held to just 33 hits and a .257 OBP against the hottest offense in the league, but Bryan Woo's 2 QS and Mason Miller's 4 NSVH are legitimately valuable pieces; dropping McGreevy without replacing him leaves the rotation dangerously thin [SAME]
14. **Benchwarmers** | Michael King's 0.82 ERA was beautiful and Bellinger looked lively, but 4 HR, 45 K, and a paper-thin power profile make this team entirely luck-dependent — a matchup against Buttville offers a winnable path but the ceiling is still very low [DOWN]

---

## Waiver Wire

There were no notable waiver wire moves of consequence this week. The league's managers, by and large, stood pat — with the notable exception of the **Albuquerque Bombers**, who made four additions in a clear signal of managerial ambition, and the **Canberra Wombats**, whose decision to drop Roki Sasaki in favor of JR Ritchie is the kind of move that will be evaluated, fairly or not, entirely by what happens next. In a league where roster construction is increasingly separating the contenders from the rest, the absence of significant waiver activity across most clubs is itself a form of information.
